ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai-style stir-fried prawn vermicelli; closest parent 'stir-fried noodle dish' (FOODON_00002473) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (noodle dish, stir-fried) used as closest parent; no specific Thai prawn vermicelli entry exists in FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for this specific dish; SNOMED CT does not catalogue individual prepared Asian noodle dishes at this level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Thai stir-fried prawn vermicelli is approximately 300 g, typically presented as a plate of wok-fried rice vermicelli with 4–6 medium prawns, bean sprouts, egg, and aromatics.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Bee Hoon with Prawns — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo entry for 'Fried Bee Hoon with Prawns' used as the primary nutrient anchor. Thai-style seasoning (fish sauce, chilli, lime) adds modest sodium and minor macro variation; values adjusted upward for sodium by approximately 10% to reflect Thai-style condiment profile. Core macros remain within the HPB reference range for prawn bee hoon dishes. USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) used to cross-check prawn and rice vermicelli component values.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice vermicelli / rice noodle analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai-style stir-fried prawn vermicelli. Rice vermicelli (bee hoon) has a published GI of approximately 53 (low) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Stir-frying with protein (prawns, egg) and fat (oil) further attenuates glycaemic response; GI of 53 is therefore a conservative upper-bound estimate. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 53.4) / 100 ≈ 28.3, rounded to 28.4 for a 300 g serving providing approximately 53.4 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request reduced-sauce preparation, as a standard 300 g serving provides approximately 1440 mg sodium, representing 60–72% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycaemic load from rice vermicelli carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: The dish has a low GI (approximately 53) due to rice vermicelli and protein co-ingestion, but the glycaemic load per standard serving is moderate (approximately 28); individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion to 150–200 g and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat from stir-frying oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (approximately 1440 mg per serving) and moderate fat from wok oil are relevant cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should request reduced-oil, reduced-sauce preparation and limit serving size to 150–200 g.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Stir-Fried Prawn Vermicelli (泰式炒虾条, Tai Shi Chao Xia Tiao) is a wok-fried rice vermicelli dish prepared in the Thai culinary style and commonly served at Chinese-run coffeeshops (kopitiams) and casual restaurants in Singapore. The dish features thin rice vermicelli noodles (bee hoon) stir-fried at high heat in a wok with fresh or frozen prawns, beaten egg, bean sprouts, spring onions, and garlic, seasoned with a combination of fish sauce, oyster sauce, light soy sauce, and fresh chilli or chilli paste, finished with a squeeze of lime. The Thai-style preparation distinguishes it from standard Singaporean fried bee hoon by its use of fish sauce and lime, which impart a tangy, savoury, and mildly spicy flavour profile. The texture is characterised by slightly chewy vermicelli strands, tender prawns, and crisp bean sprouts. Nutritionally, a standard 300 g serving provides approximately 414 kcal, 21.6 g protein, 13.5 g fat, and 53.4 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium (approximately 1440 mg per serving) due to multiple fermented condiments, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycaemic index is estimated at 53 (low) based on rice vermicelli reference values, though the glycaemic load per serving is moderate at approximately 28, relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. The dish is dairy-free and generally halal when prepared without pork-derived sauces. It is a good source of complete protein from prawns and egg, and provides moderate amounts of B vitamins, phosphorus, and potassium.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo entry exists for 'Thai-style stir-fried prawn vermicelli' as a named dish. Nutrient values are adapted from the HPB Singapore Foodo entry for 'Fried Bee Hoon with Prawns', which is the closest approved primary source match. Sodium adjusted upward to reflect Thai-style condiment profile. GI is an analogous estimate based on published rice vermicelli GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish category. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish category. 3. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish category. 4. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in stir-fried vegetable-oil-based dishes per HPB and USDA reference data. 5. The food name in the input JSON is the Chinese name 泰式炒虾条; canonical English name 'Thai Stir-Fried Prawn Vermicelli' has been assigned as food_name per Veritas rules. 6. GI of 53 is a conservative analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli; actual GI may be lower due to protein and fat co-ingestion during stir-frying. 7. Halal status is conditional on the restaurant using halal-certified sauces; Keming Bing Sat 克茗冰室 is a Chinese kopitiam and halal certification status should be verified independently. 8. Monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for vegetable oil and prawn; not directly reported in HPB Foodo.

improvement_actions_taken: Assigned canonical English food name 'Thai Stir-Fried Prawn Vermicelli' from Chinese input name 泰式炒虾条.
Added Chinese traditional form 泰式炒蝦條.
Added Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and Thai local names to satisfy multilingual requirements for a Singapore-served dish with Thai culinary origin.
Populated all nutrient fields using HPB Singapore Foodo adapted entry for Fried Bee Hoon with Prawns with sodium adjustment for Thai condiment profile.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) with explicit source-not-reported justification in notes.
Added GI analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 for rice vermicelli.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list for perfect consistency before finalising.
Set ontology match types to 'closest_parent' or 'none' only; no invented codes used.
Added zero-macro justification note for trans_fat_g = 0.0 (confirmed trace/negligible).
